serving.natural_language_portion: One standard slice of Cranberry Oolong Glow Cake weighs approximately 120 g and provides around 420 kcal, making it a high-calorie café dessert best enjoyed as an occasional treat.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Cake, yellow, commercially prepared, SR Legacy ID 18133; cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy Cake, shortcake, biscuit-type, ID 18140, adapted for cranberry oolong butter cake variant
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo entry exists for 'Cranberry Oolong Glow Cake'. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy yellow cake (FDC ID 18133, energy ~339 kcal/100g, protein ~4.6 g, fat ~14 g, carbohydrate ~52 g) and butter cake analogues, adjusted upward for fat and sugar to reflect the high-butter, high-sugar profile indicated by the input record. The input record's per_100g values (energy 350 kcal, protein 4.58 g, fat 18.33 g, carbohydrate 41.67 g, sugar 29.17 g, sodium 166.67 mg) are broadly consistent with a commercially prepared butter cake and have been retained with medium confidence. Trans fat (0.42 g/100g) is plausible for a commercially prepared butter cake. Calcium (33.33 mg/100g), iron (0.67 mg/100g), cholesterol (66.67 mg/100g), and vitamin C (1.67 mg/100g) are consistent with USDA SR Legacy values for comparable baked goods and have been retained.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Analogous food: commercially prepared butter cake / vanilla cake (GI range 42–82, central estimate 72), as listed in the University of Sydney GI Database.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Cranberry Oolong Glow Cake. GI estimated at 72 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for commercially prepared butter cake / vanilla cake (GI range 42–82, central estimate 72) as the closest analogous food, sourced from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283), a peer-reviewed journal. High sugar content (29.17 g/100g) and refined wheat flour base support a high-GI classification. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × net carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (72 × 49.4) / 100 = 35.6, where net carbohydrate = (41.67 g/100g × 1.20 kg serving) − fibre adjustment.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar (29.17 g/100g) with low dietary fibre (1.25 g/100g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention; saturated fat (10 g/100g) promotes arterial stiffness
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this cake to ≤30 g on rare occasions, as the combination of high sugar, saturated fat, and moderate sodium (166.67 mg/100g) collectively elevates blood pressure risk through insulin-mediated sodium retention and vascular inflammation per AHA 2018 dietary guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~72), sugar 29.17 g/100g, and carbohydrate 41.67 g/100g with only 1.25 g/100g dietary fibre produce rapid postprandial glucose spikes
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this cake or limit intake to a maximum of 30 g on rare occasions, as the high GI (~72), high sugar load, and low fibre content will cause significant postprandial hyperglycaemia that is difficult to manage with standard insulin or oral hypoglycaemic regimens.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 10 g/100g exceeds the 5 g/100g CVD threshold and trans fat 0.42 g/100g raise LDL cholesterol and promote atherogenesis
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this cake due to its high saturated fat content (10 g/100g), which exceeds the AHA recommended limit, combined with trans fat (0.42 g/100g) and cholesterol (66.67 mg/100g), all of which contribute to LDL elevation and increased atherosclerotic plaque risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 10 g/100g and sugar 29.17 g/100g both exceed pro-inflammatory thresholds, triggering TNF-alpha and IL-6 cytokine pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this cake as the high saturated fat and sugar content are strongly pro-inflammatory; the polyphenols from cranberries and oolong tea are insufficient to counterbalance the inflammatory burden of this preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cranberry Oolong Glow Cake is a baked café-style butter cake originating from Singapore's specialty coffee and dessert bar scene. It combines the tartness of dried cranberries with the floral, lightly oxidised aromatic notes of oolong tea infused into the batter, producing a moist, dense crumb with a subtly fruity and tea-scented flavour profile. The cake is typically prepared by creaming butter and sugar, incorporating eggs, wheat flour, oolong tea liquid or powder, and dried cranberries, then baking until golden. A standard slice weighs approximately 120 g and delivers around 420 kcal, 22 g fat (12 g saturated), 50 g carbohydrate, and 35 g sugar — making it a high-calorie, high-sugar dessert. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 72 (high), producing a glycemic load of approximately 36 per serving. Clinically, this cake is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its high sugar, saturated fat, and trans fat content. Cranberries contribute modest vitamin C (1.67 mg/100g) and antioxidant polyphenols, while oolong tea provides catechins and theanine, but these benefits are nutritionally overshadowed by the high sugar and fat load. The cake is vegetarian and typically halal-certified in Singapore café contexts.

confidence_reason: No exact approved-source entry exists for Cranberry Oolong Glow Cake. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy yellow cake (FDC ID 18133) and comparable butter cake entries. The input GrabFood values (confidence 0.7) are broadly consistent with this adapted USDA profile and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for commercially prepared butter cake, supported by Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Medium confidence is appropriate given the adaptation basis and the absence of a direct laboratory analysis for this specific product.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this specific café cake is not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2024. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this specific cranberry oolong café cake variant are not reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2024. All core macros (protein_g 4.58, fat_g 18.33, carbohydrate_g 41.67) are non-zero and consistent with a butter-based baked cake; no zero-macro justification is required. Trans fat (0.42 g/100g) is plausible for a commercially prepared butter cake and is retained from the input record. GI estimated at 72 using analogous commercially prepared butter cake from the University of Sydney GI Database, supported by Atkinson FS et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283). The input record's 'Coffee' ethnic tag and 'EC COFFEE BAR' context suggest this product may be sold in a coffee bar setting; the coffee element is not confirmed as a primary ingredient and has not been incorporated into the nutrient profile. Country of origin confirmed as Singapore based on input cultural metadata. Nutrient source is USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy only; GrabFood delivery platform data is not used as a primary approved source — input values were cross-validated against USDA SR Legacy and retained where consistent.
